title: Small business buckles as late payments hit six-year high
full_text:
Payment defaults have surged to dangerous levels not seen since 2020, with experts warning of structural vulnerabilities threatening business survival across Australia.
Higher interest rates, inflation and energy prices have all contributed to the six-year high of late payments for businesses, pushing up operating expenses.
The April Business Risk Index (BRI) from credit reporting agency CreditorWatch shows cashflow stress – which is a key forward indicator for insolvencies – is spreading across Australian businesses.
Financial stress is also emerging earlier in the cycle, with deteriorating payment behaviour pointing to structural vulnerabilities rather than a typical cyclic slowdown.
CreditorWatch said pressure was uneven across the economy, with hospitality, construction, transport, manufacturing and other cost and demand-sensitive sectors showing the highest rates of insolvencies, late payments and invoice defaults.
Overall payment arrears were at their highest level since January 2020. According to the index the rate of B2B trade payment arrears of more that 60 days was just over 6 per cent and 90 days about 4.8 per cent.
CreditorWatch chief executive Patrick Coghlan said the April data showed that the business risk story has moved from macro pressure to measurable cashflow behaviour.
“We are not seeing a sudden collapse in business conditions, but we are seeing a less forgiving trading environment,” he said.
“More invoices are sliding beyond 60 days overdue, and the stress is concentrated in sectors central to household spending, supply chains and small business employment.”
The industries that have seen the largest increases in payment defaults have relatively greater exposure to both interest rates and energy prices.
Food and beverage services had the highest rate of invoices more than 60 days overdue at 11.37 per cent, followed by electricity, gas, water and waste services at 8.16 per cent, rental, hiring and real estate services at 7.51 per cent, construction at 7.15 per cent, transport, postal and warehousing at 7.09 per cent and retail trade at 6.59 per cent.
CreditorWatch chief economist Ivan Colhoun said that Australian economic data to date shows only a slight deterioration in business conditions, but much bigger impacts were on confidence and input costs.
“The surge in input costs and retail prices add to prior cost of living and cost of doing business pressures and can be expected to lead to weaker business conditions in coming months unless the Strait of Hormuz reopens relatively soon,” he said.
“The third successive interest rate increase by the RBA to address Australia’s pre-existing above-target inflation will add additional pressure to businesses and consumers in coming months.”
CreditorWatch’s ATO default data for April shows an increasing number of businesses are falling behind on their tax payments. Three of the four highest new inflows since the increase in enforcement post Covid, have been recorded in the past four months increasing the rick on insolvency.
Tax-debt pressure remains heavily concentrated among smaller and less-structured business entities. Sole traders account for 54 per cent of outstanding tax debt defaults greater than $100,000.
Mr Colhoun said higher fuel costs had a significant impact.
“These prices could reverse relatively quickly if a peace agreement is signed soon, but by the same token, energy prices might rise sharply further were the Strait of Hormuz to remain closed for an extended period,” he said.
“This uncertainty is of course very difficult for businesses.
“Encouragingly, the outlook for the Australian economy seemed to be improving ahead of the Iran conflict, though recent interest rate rises will no doubt be a headwind across the second half of the year.
“If an agreement to reopen the Strait can be reached soon, there is a reasonable prospect that these more favourable economic conditions can be re-established.”
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.

summary: Late payments for Australian businesses have reached their highest level in six years, driven by rising interest rates, inflation, and energy costs. This has led to increased financial stress, particularly in sectors like hospitality, construction, and transport, raising concerns about business survival.
author: Chris Herde
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Payment defaults at a six-year high.
April Business Risk Index shows over 6% of B2B payments are over 60 days late.
Sectors most affected include food services, construction, and transport.
Higher costs and interest rates are increasing financial pressure.
54% of tax debt defaults over $100,000 are from sole traders.
ticker_line: Late payments surge as businesses struggle
